question: 6. Any positive integer may be written in the form \( n=2^{k}(2 l+1) \). Let \( a_{n} \) \( =e^{-k} \) and \( b_{n}=a_{1} a_{2} a_{3} \cdots a_{n} \). Prove that \( \Sigma b_{n} \) converges.
solution:
Solution. It is clear that \( a_{n}=e^{0}=1 \) if \( n \) is odd and \( a_{n} \leq e^{-1} \) if \( n \) is even. Therefore
\[
b_{2 k}=a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{2 k} \leq e^{-k},
\]
and
\[
b_{2 k+1} \leq e^{-k}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots b_{2 k} & <b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+b_{2 k+1} \\
& \leq 1+2 e^{-1}+2 e^{-2}+\cdots+2 e^{-2 k}<1+\frac{2 e^{-1}}{1-e^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the partial sums of \( \Sigma b_{n} \) are bounded. Since the series has positive terms, it converges.

descriptive_long.question: 6. Any positive integer may be written in the form \( posintnum=2^{twopowerexp}(2 oddpartpar+1) \). Let \( aseqelem \) \( =e^{-twopowerexp} \) and \( bseqelem=aseqfirst aseqsecond aseqthird \cdots aseqelem \). Prove that \( \Sigma bseqelem \) converges.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. It is clear that \( aseqelem=e^{0}=1 \) if \( posintnum \) is odd and \( aseqelem \leq e^{-1} \) if \( posintnum \) is even. Therefore
\[
bseqeven=a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{2\,twopowerexp} \leq e^{-twopowerexp},
\]
and
\[
bseqoddnext \leq e^{-twopowerexp}
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots bseqeven & <b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+bseqoddnext \\
& \leq 1+2 e^{-1}+2 e^{-2}+\cdots+2 e^{-2\,twopowerexp}<1+\frac{2 e^{-1}}{1-e^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus the partial sums of \( \Sigma bseqelem \) are bounded. Since the series has positive terms, it converges.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: Any positive integer may be written in the form \( timberwolf=2^{marigolds}(2 sandpiper+1) \). Let \( cranberries=e^{-marigolds} \) and \( cornstarch=blacksmith toothbrush peppercorn \cdots cranberries \). Prove that \( \Sigma cornstarch \) converges.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. It is clear that \( cranberries=e^{0}=1 \) if \( timberwolf \) is odd and \( cranberries \leq e^{-1} \) if \( timberwolf \) is even. Therefore
\[
raincloud=blacksmith toothbrush \cdots a_{2 marigolds} \leq e^{-marigolds},
\]
and
\[
dragonfly \leq e^{-marigolds}
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
 b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots raincloud & <b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+dragonfly \\
 & \leq 1+2 e^{-1}+2 e^{-2}+\cdots+2 e^{-2 marigolds}<1+\frac{2 e^{-1}}{1-e^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the partial sums of \( \Sigma cornstarch \) are bounded. Since the series has positive terms, it converges.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
<<<
6. Any positive integer may be written in the form \( fractionvalue=2^{logarithm}(2 negative+1) \). Let \( largeseries =e^{-logarithm} \) and \( quotientseq=giganticone gigantictwo giganticthree \cdots largeseries \). Prove that \( \Sigma quotientseq \) converges.
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
<<<
Solution. It is clear that \( largeseries=e^{0}=1 \) if \( fractionvalue \) is odd and \( largeseries \leq e^{-1} \) if \( fractionvalue \) is even. Therefore
\[
quotienteven=giganticone gigantictwo \cdots largeseries \leq e^{-logarithm},
\]
and
\[
quotientodd \leq e^{-logarithm}
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+quotienteven & <b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+quotientodd \\
& \leq 1+2 e^{-1}+2 e^{-2}+\cdots+2 e^{-2 logarithm}<1+\frac{2 e^{-1}}{1-e^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus the partial sums of \( \Sigma quotientseq \) are bounded. Since the series has positive terms, it converges.
>>>

garbled_string.question: 6. Any positive integer may be written in the form \( qzxwvtnp=2^{hjgrksla}(2 mdfqplzo+1) \). Let \( rcptebha =e^{-hjgrksla} \) and \( kvusymni=sblqtrwo pfkxajcm yvnhqzig \cdots rcptebha \). Prove that \( \Sigma kvusymni \) converges.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. It is clear that \( rcptebha=e^{0}=1 \) if \( qzxwvtnp \) is odd and \( rcptebha \leq e^{-1} \) if \( qzxwvtnp \) is even. Therefore
\[
wdrxoful=sblqtrwo pfkxajcm \cdots a_{2 hjgrksla} \leq e^{-hjgrksla},
\]
and
\[
tnmqzsky \leq e^{-hjgrksla}
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots wdrxoful & <b_{1}+b_{2}+\cdots+tnmqzsky \\
& \leq 1+2 e^{-1}+2 e^{-2}+\cdots+2 e^{-2 hjgrksla}<1+\frac{2 e^{-1}}{1-e^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus the partial sums of \( \Sigma kvusymni \) are bounded. Since the series has positive terms, it converges.

kernel_variant.question:
Every positive integer admits a unique factorisation  
 n = 2^{\alpha (n)} 3^{\beta (n)} m with gcd(m, 6)=1.  
Define a_n = 7^{-\alpha (n)} 5^{-\beta (n)} and b_n = \prod _{j=1}^{n} a_j.  
Prove that the series \sum _{n=1}^{\infty } b_n converges and give an explicit numerical upper bound for its sum.
kernel_variant.solution:
(\approx  60 words, original style)  
Write n as above and set a_n, b_n accordingly.  
Note that a_n=1 if 6\nmid n; a_n\leq 7^{-1} when 2|n, 3\nmid n; a_n\leq 5^{-1} when 3|n, 2\nmid n; a_n\leq 35^{-1} when 6|n.  
Among 1,\ldots ,n there are \lfloor n/2\rfloor  even numbers and \lfloor n/3\rfloor  multiples of 3, whence  

 b_n \leq  7^{-\lfloor n/2\rfloor } 5^{-\lfloor n/3\rfloor } \leq  35\cdot (35)^{-n/6}.  

Thus \sum  b_n \leq  35 \sum _{n\geq 1}(35)^{-n/6}=35\cdot (35^{-1/6})/(1-35^{-1/6})<\infty , so the series converges.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
